nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline littoral zone of Lake Malawi; found among rocks and boulders at shallow to mid depths, grazing aufwuchs from rock surfaces

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks and boulders creating caves and crevices
rockwork to the back and sides to define territories
minimal or no planting (plants will be uprooted)
sandy substrate for egg-spitting and natural behavior

diet.notes: Primary diet is aufwuchs — the algae-microorganism mat scraped from rocks in the wild. In captivity: spirulina-based flakes and pellets form the core diet. Supplement with blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini, peas) and algae wafers. Occasional high-protein frozen foods (brine shrimp, mysis) are acceptable in small quantities but a protein-heavy diet is associated with Malawi bloat — a fatal digestive condition. Avoid beef heart or high-fat mammalian proteins.

goodWith: other large mbuna cichlids of similar aggression (e.g., Melanochromis johanni, Pseudotropheus demasoni with care)
robust Haplochromis-type cichlids in large tanks
fast-moving mid-size mbuna as dither fish
avoidWith: other Melanochromis species (extreme aggression, interbreeding risk)
any fish that cannot hold its own against constant harassment

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen showing classic black-and-white striped coloration. FishBase species photo.
Female/juvenile showing golden yellow body with black and white lateral stripes; freely licensed.

summary: One of Lake Malawi's most aggressive mbuna cichlids. Males are striking black-and-white striped; females golden yellow. A maternal mouthbrooder best kept in species-dominant mbuna-only tanks with heavy rockwork, experienced keepers, and a strong 1:4+ male-to-female ratio.
careNotes:
The Auratus Cichlid (Melanochromis auratus) is widely considered among the most aggressive of all mbuna cichlids, and caution is strongly warranted even in dedicated African cichlid setups. Aquarium Co-Op and Practical Fishkeeping both cite it as wholly unsuitable for community aquaria. In their natural Lake Malawi habitat, auratus graze aufwuchs — the biofilm of algae and microorganisms coating rocky surfaces — and their entire behavioral ecology revolves around defending rocky territory.

Sexual dichromatism is extreme and striking: females and juveniles are golden yellow with a thin black stripe and a white dorsal stripe; mature males reverse this palette, becoming predominantly black with white/yellow horizontal stripes. This colour reversal at maturity sometimes catches aquarists off guard when purchasing juveniles that later become far more aggressive adult males.

Tank requirements: a minimum of 200 L (120 cm length) for a breeding group, though 300+ L is strongly preferred. Rockwork should fill the back two-thirds of the tank to create numerous caves and broken lines of sight — this diffuses aggression by preventing any single male from patrolling the entire tank. Overstock slightly compared with other cichlid species to further dilute targeted aggression. A ratio of one male to four or more females is recommended; multiple males in the same tank will often result in the death of subordinate males unless the tank is very large and heavily decorated. pH should be maintained at 7.8–8.6 with moderately hard to hard water (gH 10–25, kH 6–16), replicating Lake Malawi's naturally buffered chemistry. Regular water changes of 30–50% weekly help maintain low nitrates, which are especially important given the mbuna susceptibility to Malawi bloat.

Do not mix with peaceful community species. Compatible tankmates are limited to other robust mbuna with similar aggression levels and notably different colouration to avoid mistaken conspecific rivalry. Melanochromis species should not be mixed together due to extreme aggression and hybridization risk.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning occurs on flat rock surfaces or cleared sandy patches; the female picks up the fertilised eggs in her mouth and broods them for approximately 21–28 days. Clutch sizes typically range from 10–30 fry. The female should ideally be moved to a separate holding tank toward the end of her brooding period to protect both mother and fry. Fry are large enough to accept crushed spirulina flake, baby brine shrimp, and micro-pellets immediately on release. Males are aggressive toward females outside of spawning; maintain a high female-to-male ratio and provide dense rockwork cover. Brood interference (stripping the female) is sometimes practiced by experienced breeders at day 14–18 to artificially raise fry away from tankmate harassment.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanochromis auratus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Malawi rocky littoral zone), adult size (males to 11 cm), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 24–26°C, hardness 215–447 ppm / 12–25 dH), diet (aufwuchs/herbivore), behaviour (extremely aggressive, mbuna territory defence), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day brokd, 10–30 fry), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), and minimum tank size guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Melanochromis auratus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 11 cm TL), temperature range (24–28°C), native range (Lake Malawi), diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes). Habitat noted as rocky shores, depth 1–20 m.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care
additional.notes: Trade/hobbyist source. Confirmed aggressive temperament and unsuitability as community fish; spirulina-based diet and Malawi bloat risk from protein-rich foods; minimum tank size (55 gal / ~208 L) for mbuna groups; hard alkaline water parameters; heavy rockwork recommendation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Melanochromis auratus profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist reference. Confirmed extreme aggression rating, male colour reversal (golden juvenile to black adult), 1:4 male-to-female ratio recommendation, maternal mouthbrooder reproduction, and warning against keeping with less-assertive species. Noted as among the most aggressive mbuna available in the trade.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Melanochromis auratus species article
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community source. Additional detail on sexual dichromatism reversal at maturity, aggression even within mbuna communities, tank overcrowding strategy to diffuse aggression, and depth range (1–20 m in Lake Malawi rocky habitat).

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; females brood 10–30 fry for ~21–28 days. Separate brooding females to a holding tank at day 14–18 to protect mother and fry. Fry accept crushed spirulina flake and baby brine shrimp from release. High female-to-male ratio (4:1+) essential.
